comment_text.sn9.8:2.2: For sabhā as a gathering-place for (disreputable) travellers, see Manu 9.264.
comment_text.sn9.8:3.1: For the unique paccūhā see Sanskrit prativyūha, “an army arrayed against an enemy” or else “echo, reverberation”. It calls back to the previous line, “between you and me”.
comment_text.sn9.8:4.2: According to Haṁsadeva’s 13th century Mṛgapakṣiśāstra, the “wind-deer” (vātamiga, Sanskrit vātamṛga) is fast, skilled at escaping weapons and vanishing from sight. Dwelling in deep forests, it is lean with long legs and branched antlers. It makes an appearance also in Ja 14 and Tha Ap 482.

root_text.sn9.8:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ aññataro bhikkhu kosalesu viharati aññatarasmiṁ vanasaṇḍe.
root_text.sn9.8:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena so bhikkhu aññatarasmiṁ kule ativelaṁ ajjhogāḷhappatto viharati.
root_text.sn9.8:1.3: Atha kho yā tasmiṁ vanasaṇḍe adhivatthā devatā tassa bhikkhuno anukampikā atthakāmā taṁ bhikkhuṁ saṁvejetukāmā yā tasmiṁ kule kulagharaṇī, tassā vaṇṇaṁ abhinimminitvā yena so bhikkhu tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā taṁ bhikkhuṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:

translation_text.sn9.8:1.1: At one time one of the monks was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove.
translation_text.sn9.8:1.2: Now at that time that monk had become too closely involved in the affairs of a certain family.
translation_text.sn9.8:1.3: The deity haunting that forest had sympathy for that monk, wanting what’s best for him. So, wanting to stir him up, they manifested in the appearance of the mistress of that family, approached the monk, and addressed him in verse:
translation_text.sn9.8:2.4: what’s going on between you and me.”
translation_text.sn9.8:3.2: that a fervent ascetic must endure.
translation_text.sn9.8:3.3: They ought not be dismayed by that,
translation_text.sn9.8:3.4: for that’s not what defiles you.
translation_text.sn9.8:4.1: If you’re startled by every little sound,
translation_text.sn9.8:4.2: like a wind-deer in the wood,
